title: ML Feature Store — System Design
description: Design a feature store: the central system that computes ML features once and serves them consistently for both training (offline, historical) and inference (online, low-latency). The themes are the offline/online dual store, point-in-time-correct training data, train/serve consistency, and feature freshness.

nodes.label: ML Engineer
nodes.description: Defines feature transformations once; uses them in training and serving.

nodes.label: Feature Registry
nodes.description: Central definitions: each feature's name, source, transform, owner.

nodes.label: Batch Compute (Offline)
nodes.description: Computes features over historical data on a schedule.

nodes.label: Stream Compute (Online)
nodes.description: Computes fresh features from event streams in near-real-time.

nodes.label: Data Sources
nodes.description: Warehouses, event streams, and operational DBs feature inputs come from.

nodes.label: Offline Store (Warehouse)
nodes.description: Historical feature values with timestamps for training.

nodes.label: Online Store (Low-Latency)
nodes.description: Latest feature values keyed by entity for serving.

nodes.label: Training Data API
nodes.description: Builds point-in-time-correct training datasets from the offline store.

nodes.label: Online Serving API
nodes.description: Returns features for an entity at inference time (ms).

nodes.label: Model / Inference Service
nodes.description: Consumes features online; trained on offline features.

nodes.label: Materialization Job
nodes.description: Loads computed features into the online store; keeps it fresh.

nodes.label: Monitoring / Drift
nodes.description: Tracks freshness, drift, and serving/training skew.

functional: Define a feature (source + transformation) once, in a central registry.
Serve features online at inference time with low latency, keyed by entity.
Provide historical feature values for training, joined correctly to labels.
Compute features in batch (historical) and streaming (fresh).
Reuse and share features across teams/models; discover them.
nonFunctional: Train/serve consistency: training and serving use the same feature definitions/values logic (no skew).
Point-in-time correctness: training data uses feature values as of each event's time (no leakage).
Low online serving latency (single-digit ms) under high QPS.
Freshness: online features reflect recent data within an acceptable lag.
Scale to thousands of features across many entities and models.

capacity.label: Entities
capacity.note: Several entity types; sizing the online keyspace and shard count.

capacity.label: Features
capacity.note: Shared across ~100 models/teams; 20-200 fetched per request.

capacity.label: Online reads
capacity.note: 1-20 KB feature vector per request via a single multi-get on the inference path.

capacity.label: Online writes
capacity.note: Plus nightly batch refresh of ~1B entity-feature rows.

capacity.label: Offline / training
capacity.note: 2-year history retention; multi-TB warehouse scans per training set.

capacity.label: Freshness classes
capacity.note: Per-feature SLO: real-time counts seconds-fresh; lifetime stats daily.

capacity.label: Online store size
capacity.note: Latest values only; SSD-backed KV with hot keys in memory.

capacity.label: Derived: read bandwidth
capacity.note: 100k QPS * 1-20 KB vector before replication/cache; pushes SSD-backed KV with a hot-key in-memory tier and a single multi-get per request to bound fanout.

capacity.label: Derived: write bandwidth
capacity.note: 200k updates/sec * 1-20 KB; entity-key sharding spreads it so no single shard takes the full stream write rate.

capacity.label: Derived: online footprint
capacity.note: 1-2 TB hot set * 3 replicas. Entity-key sharding sized so each shard's working set fits the in-memory tier; replicas absorb hot-key reads and shard loss.

capacity.label: Derived: offline + stream state
capacity.note: Warehouse partitioned so a 1B-row PIT join scans only relevant feature/entity/time ranges; stream operator state bounded by active entities * windowed features, checkpointed to durable storage.

capacity.label: Derived: backfill limits
capacity.note: Large backfills throttled/isolated from stream materialization so they never starve online freshness. If QPS or vector size goes 10x, the read-bandwidth and footprint estimates scale linearly — add shards/replicas and lean harder on the hot-key cache before the offline path changes at all.

api.description: Register a feature definition (source + transform) used by both training and serving.

Fetch latest feature values for an entity at inference time, with freshness/version metadata and partial-result policy. Pass a model feature set (which pins versions) or explicit featureRefs; maxStalenessMs marks values older than the bound as stale.

Submit an asynchronous point-in-time-correct training-dataset job over an entity+timestamp+label list. A 1B-row PIT join scans multi-TB of warehouse history, so this returns a job handle immediately rather than a finished dataset. idempotencyKey makes a retried submission return the same jobId instead of starting a duplicate scan.

messages.label: submit job: entities + timestamps + label + feature set

api.description: Poll an async training-dataset job. When done it returns the output URI plus the manifest that makes the dataset auditable: the exact feature versions and the source snapshot/watermark the PIT join read, so the dataset is reproducible and a model knows precisely which inputs produced it.

dataModel.name: feature_definition
dataModel.note: Central versioned contract reused by offline + online compute; a definition change is a new version.

dataModel.name: feature_value_history (offline warehouse)
dataModel.note: Timestamped history for point-in-time joins; event_ts vs ingested_at separates value validity from arrival, so late/backfilled data does not leak.

fields.name: event_ts
fields.type: when the value was valid (join key)

dataModel.name: online_feature_value (kv)
dataModel.note: Latest value per entity+feature for low-latency serving; carries freshness metadata the serving API surfaces.

dataModel.name: model_feature_binding
dataModel.note: Pins a model version to the exact feature versions it trained on, so a later definition change cannot silently change inputs.

dataModel.name: model_feature_set
dataModel.note: Resolves modelFeatureSet=ranker@v7 into a pinned policy the serving layer applies before reading the online store: which features are required, their defaults, and how stale each may be.

dataModel.name: model_feature_set_member
dataModel.note: One row per feature in a feature set: pins the version and the per-feature serving policy (required vs optional, safe default, staleness bound).

dataModel.name: training_dataset_job
dataModel.note: Async job + manifest for a 1B-row PIT training-set build: status/progress, the output URI, and the exact feature versions and source snapshot/watermark that produced it (auditable, reproducible, idempotent by key).

dataModel.name: access_policy + audit_event + deletion_request
dataModel.note: Governance is enforced, not just annotated: access_policy authorizes a (team/model, feature) read by classification; audit_event logs who read/changed what; deletion_request propagates a user erasure to offline history and online values.

dataModel.name: materialization_job
dataModel.note: Control-plane state making rebuildable online state safe: checkpointed, idempotent, with a publish boundary so partial refreshes never become visible.

patterns.name: Central feature registry
patterns.what: Define each feature once with a source + transform; everything references the definition.
patterns.whenToUse: Sharing/governing features across teams and avoiding duplicate logic.

patterns.name: Dual offline/online stores
patterns.what: An offline store (historical, for training) and an online store (latest, for serving), fed from the same definitions.
patterns.whenToUse: Features needed for both bulk training and low-latency inference.

patterns.name: Point-in-time correctness
patterns.what: Join feature values as-of each training event's timestamp, never future values.
patterns.whenToUse: Building training data without label leakage.

patterns.name: Train/serve consistency
patterns.what: Use identical feature logic offline and online so the model sees the same inputs.
patterns.whenToUse: Any production ML system (skew silently degrades quality).

patterns.name: Materialization + freshness
patterns.what: Materialize computed features into the online store; streaming for fresh, batch for the rest.
patterns.whenToUse: Keeping serving features current at acceptable cost.

patterns.name: Feature monitoring (drift/skew)
patterns.what: Monitor freshness, distribution drift, and online/offline skew.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Each Model Computes Features Ad Hoc
description: Start with how teams actually begin: there is no feature store. Each model's training pipeline computes its features directly from the raw sources in its own code (a SQL query, a Spark job), and each serving service re-implements the same logic to compute features at inference time.

It works for one model, but it doesn't scale across teams or stay correct. Take user_7d_clicks (a user's clicks in the last 7 days), the example we follow through the whole design. The training pipeline computes it in batch SQL over warehouse history; the serving service recomputes it from a recent-events table at request time. The two implementations are 'the same' feature, but they inevitably drift — train/serve skew that silently degrades the model. Meanwhile every team re-derives popular features like this slightly differently, duplicating work, and nobody can discover or reuse what already exists.
So the ad-hoc approach motivates a central place to define each feature once — a registry — and dedicated offline/online stores fed from that single definition.

view.caption: The model/inference service computes its features ad hoc by reading directly from the raw data sources at request time.

steps.decisionPrompt: The training pipeline and the serving service each compute 'user_7d_clicks' in their own code. What guarantees the two implementations agree?
whyNow: Seeing each model compute features ad hoc makes the skew and duplication concrete — the registry, dual stores, point-in-time joins, and consistency guarantees all exist to fix exactly this.

recap.after: Each model/pipeline computes its own features directly from raw sources, in its own code.
recap.newRisk: Training and serving logic diverge (skew), feature logic is duplicated across teams, and nothing is reusable or discoverable.

traps.trap: Computing features in each pipeline/service independently.
traps.why: Two implementations of the same feature drift, causing train/serve skew; logic is duplicated and impossible to reuse.
traps.instead: Define each feature once in a central registry that drives both offline and online computation.

steps.title: 2. Define Features Once (Registry)
description: The core idea: define each feature once — its entity, source data, and transformation (e.g. 'count of a user's clicks in the last 7 days') — in a central registry. Both the training and serving paths reference this single definition, so the logic can't diverge.

Without this, teams reimplement the same feature differently in training pipelines and serving code — the classic source of train/serve skew. The registry also enables discovery and reuse (one team's feature is another's input) and governance (ownership, access control, lineage).

The definition is a versioned contract, not just a name. Each feature carries a lifecycle state (draft -> reviewed -> active -> deprecated -> retired) with owner approval and backward-compatibility checks, and changing the transform mints a new version. Models pin the feature versions they trained on, so a later definition change can't silently alter their inputs — this is what makes train/serve consistency survive feature evolution (the scale step then operates this contract across many teams). The definition is the contract; the rest of the system computes and serves it consistently.

view.caption: An ML engineer defines a feature once in the feature registry, which hands the transform spec to both batch (offline) and stream (online) compute.

options.name: Central declarative registry (default)
options.description: Each feature is declared once (entity, source, transform) in a central catalog that drives both the batch and stream compute paths.
pros: One definition (entity, source, transform) drives both batch and stream compute, so 'user_7d_clicks' can't diverge across train and serve.
A versioned contract with a lifecycle (draft/reviewed/active/deprecated/retired) and compatibility checks; models pin versions so definition changes don't break trained models.
Enables discovery, reuse, ownership, access control, and lineage across teams from a single catalog rather than scattered in pipeline code.
cons: The registry's transform DSL must be expressive enough for every feature, or teams escape it.
Adds a central component teams must adopt and keep authoritative.
A registry outage or bad migration can block all feature authoring.

view.caption: An ML engineer declares a feature in the central registry, which pushes one transform spec to both the batch and stream compute engines.

options.name: Shared transform library (SDK)
options.description: Feature logic lives as versioned code in a shared library that both training and serving import, with no separate registry service.
pros: Feature logic is normal versioned code in a shared library both training and serving import — no new service.
Full expressiveness of a general-purpose language; no DSL limits.
Familiar review/test/release flow for engineers.
cons: No central catalog — discovery and reuse depend on people reading code.
A team can pin an old library version on one side, silently reintroducing train/serve skew.
Governance (ownership, lineage, access) is not enforced anywhere central.

view.caption: An ML engineer publishes transform code into a shared SDK library that both batch and stream compute import, rather than a central registry.

options.name: Per-team ad hoc definitions
options.description: Each team writes its own feature code independently in its own pipelines, with no shared definition, catalog, or component.
pros: Zero adoption cost — each team just writes its own feature code.
Maximum local autonomy and speed for a single model.
No shared component to build or operate.
cons: Every team re-derives popular features slightly differently — duplicated work and divergent logic.
Train and serve implementations are separate, the classic source of train/serve skew.
No discovery, reuse, ownership, or lineage at all — this is the naive baseline.

view.caption: Each ML engineer wires their own transform straight into batch or stream compute, which pull historical data and event streams from the sources independently.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why define features centrally instead of computing them inside each training pipeline and serving service?

concepts.definition: A central catalog of feature definitions (source + transform) referenced everywhere.
concepts.whyItMatters: Single source of truth prevents duplicated/divergent logic and enables reuse + governance.
concepts.example: Define user_7d_clicks once; training and serving both use it.

concepts.definition: Each feature has a lifecycle (draft/reviewed/active/deprecated/retired) and a version that increments on any transform change; models pin the versions they trained on.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets feature definitions evolve without silently changing the inputs of already-trained models, preserving train/serve consistency over time.
concepts.example: Redefining user_7d_clicks mints v4; the ranker stays pinned to v3 until retrained.

whyNow: The registry is the foundation; defining features once is precisely what makes train/serve consistency achievable downstream.

recap.newRisk: Training needs bulk history; serving needs ms reads — one store can't do both well.

steps.title: 3. Dual Offline and Online Stores
description: Training and serving have opposite access patterns. Training reads large historical datasets (bulk, latency-tolerant); serving reads a few features for one entity in milliseconds. So maintain two stores fed from the same definitions.

The offline store (a warehouse) holds timestamped historical feature values for building training data. The online store (a low-latency KV/cache) holds the latest value per entity for inference. Both are computed from the registry's definitions, so they're consistent by construction.

Data-movement semantics matter once you pick which store is authoritative. If the offline store is the source of truth and online is derived, online refreshes commit behind a publish boundary so a partial refresh never becomes visible, and cache misses fall back to a default/last-known value (covered in serving). If streaming instead writes online directly for fresh features, it must compute from the same registry definition as batch so the two paths keep semantic parity. Either way the online store is rebuildable derived state. Be explicit about its consistency target: serving promises read-your-latest-materialized-value, not read-after-source-event. A feature is current as of the last committed materialization (bounded by the freshness SLO), not the instant the source event happened — promising the latter would be impossible to keep.
This dual-store design is the architectural heart of a feature store: same feature, two materializations tuned to two very different read patterns.

view.caption: Batch compute writes historical features to the offline warehouse store while stream compute writes fresh features to the low-latency online store, both fed from the data sources.

options.name: Dual offline + online stores (default)
options.description: One registry definition materializes into a warehouse for bulk training reads and a low-latency KV store for single-entity inference lookups.
pros: Each store is tuned to its access pattern: warehouse for bulk historical training reads, low-latency KV for ms inference lookups.
Both materializations come from one registry definition, so they stay logically consistent.
Training's heavy scans never contend with latency-sensitive serving traffic.
cons: Two systems to operate, keep in sync, and reason about.
A feature exists in two places, so materialization (offline -> online) must be reliable.
More moving parts than a single store.

view.caption: Batch compute lands historical features in the offline warehouse and stream compute lands fresh features in the online store, keeping training and serving on separate stores.

options.name: Single store for train and serve
options.description: Both training scans and serving lookups hit one store, so a feature lives in exactly one place with no offline/online sync.
pros: One system to build, operate, and keep consistent — no offline/online sync.
A feature lives in exactly one place; no materialization step to get wrong.
Simpler mental model for a small feature set.
cons: No single engine serves both bulk historical scans and single-entity ms lookups well.
Training scans contend with and can starve latency-sensitive inference traffic.
You end up over-provisioning one workload's hardware for the other's pattern.

view.caption: Both batch and stream compute write features into a single feature store shared by training and serving, fed from the data sources.

options.name: Online cache over offline source of truth
options.description: The offline warehouse is the source of truth and a materializer copies a hot subset into an online store that acts as a derived cache.
pros: Offline warehouse stays the single source of truth; the online layer is a derived cache.
Cache loss is a rebuild from the warehouse, not data loss.
Simpler authoring: write features once to offline, then materialize a hot subset online.
cons: Fast-changing features can't wait for batch materialization — freshness suffers without a stream path.
Cache warming and eviction must be managed so serving doesn't miss entities.
Still effectively two stores; you've just made one strictly derived.

view.caption: Batch compute writes the offline warehouse as source of truth, and a materialization job loads the latest values into the online store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why not serve training and inference from the same store?

concepts.definition: A historical store for training and a low-latency store for serving, from one definition.
concepts.whyItMatters: Each is tuned to its access pattern while staying logically consistent.
concepts.example: Warehouse holds 2 years of values; KV holds the latest per user for serving.

whyNow: The dual store is the defining structure; everything (training correctness, serving latency, freshness) is shaped by it.

recap.after: Offline (historical) + online (latest), both from the registry.
recap.newRisk: Building training data naively from history can leak future information.

steps.title: 4. Point-in-Time-Correct Training Data
description: Training joins features to labeled events. The trap: using a feature's current value for a past event leaks information the model wouldn't have had then (data leakage), inflating offline metrics and failing in production.

Point-in-time correctness fixes this: for each training row (entity, event timestamp, label), join the feature value as it was at that timestamp — an 'as-of' join against the timestamped offline store. The training data API does this join correctly so engineers don't hand-roll it (and get it wrong).

The subtle part is which timestamp. The offline store separates event time (when the value was valid) from ingested/created time (when it landed). The join must filter on event_ts <= the row's event time, but it must also respect availability: a value whose ingested_at is after the row's event time would not have been known then. Otherwise a late-arriving or backfilled value silently leaks future information even though 'value valid <= event_ts' looks correct.
This is the subtlest correctness property of a feature store and a top interview signal: the offline store must be timestamped with both event and ingestion time, and joins must respect both.

Operationally, generating a training set is a 1B-row PIT join over multi-TB of history, so the API is asynchronous: POST /v1/training-datasets returns a jobId, estimated rows, and estimated cost; the job runs admission-controlled (so one team's huge join can't starve others); and when it finishes it publishes a manifest — the output URI plus the exact feature versions and source snapshot/watermark it read. That manifest makes the dataset reproducible and auditable, and an idempotency key means a retried submit returns the same job rather than re-scanning.

view.caption: The model requests a training dataset from the training data API, which runs a point-in-time join against the offline warehouse store.

options.name: As-of join in offline store (default)
options.description: The training API joins each labeled event to the feature value as of that event's timestamp, reading the timestamped history in the offline store.
pros: For each labeled event, joins the feature value as it was at that event timestamp — no future leakage.
The training API owns the join, so engineers can't hand-roll a leaky one.
Reuses the already-timestamped offline store; no new logging infrastructure.
cons: The offline store must keep full timestamped history, which grows large.
Point-in-time joins over big history are expensive to compute.
Backfilling a new feature still requires recomputing its history.

view.caption: The model pulls training data from the training data API, which does an as-of (point-in-time) join over the offline warehouse to avoid leakage.

options.name: Log features at serving time
options.description: Every inference's exact feature vector is logged at serving time, and those logged rows become the training data directly with no point-in-time join.
pros: Records the exact feature vector the model actually saw, so train and serve match by construction.
No point-in-time join needed — the logged row IS the training row.
Eliminates a whole class of leakage and skew bugs.
cons: You can only train on features that were already being served — no backfill for new features.
Requires durable, high-volume logging of every inference's inputs.
Cold start: a brand-new feature has no logged history until it ships to serving.

view.caption: The model fetches features from the online serving API at inference, which logs the served vector to a feature log that later supplies training rows.

options.name: Snapshot latest values (naive join)
options.description: Each labeled event is joined to the feature's current value with no timestamp logic, taking whatever the value is at training time.
pros: Trivial: join each event to the feature's current value with no timestamp logic.
Cheap to compute and easy to explain.
Fine for static features that genuinely never change over time.
cons: Leaks future information into past events — offline metrics inflate, production fails.
Silently wrong: the model looks great in evaluation and degrades live.
Unacceptable for any time-varying feature like user_7d_clicks.

view.caption: The model gets training data from the training data API, which joins only current latest values from the online store, ignoring history.

steps.decisionPrompt: You're training on an event from 3 months ago. Which value of 'user_7d_clicks' do you use?

concepts.definition: Joining each training event to feature values as-of that event's timestamp.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents label/feature leakage — the model trains on what it would have actually seen.
concepts.example: Event at t -> user_7d_clicks as computed at t, not today's value.

concepts.definition: Event time is when a feature value was valid; ingested/created time is when it landed in the store.
concepts.whyItMatters: A correct as-of join must respect both, or a late-arriving/backfilled value leaks information the model couldn't have had at the event.
concepts.example: A value valid at t but ingested at t+2d must not appear in a training row whose event time is t.

concepts.definition: Dataset generation is a long-running job (jobId, status, progress) that publishes a manifest pinning the feature versions and source snapshot/watermark it read.
concepts.whyItMatters: A 1B-row PIT join is too big to return synchronously, and the manifest makes the dataset reproducible, auditable, and idempotent on retry.
concepts.example: POST /v1/training-datasets -> tdj_123; poll until succeeded, then read the output URI plus {user_7d_clicks: v3}.

whyNow: Point-in-time correctness is the non-obvious correctness crux of training data; naming leakage and as-of joins is the staff-level signal.

recap.newRisk: Serving must use the SAME feature logic, or online inputs differ from training (skew).

traps.trap: Joining current feature values to historical training events.
traps.why: Leaks future information; offline metrics look great but production fails.
traps.instead: As-of (point-in-time) joins against timestamped feature values.

traps.trap: Filtering only on event_ts and ignoring when the value was ingested.
traps.why: A late-arriving or backfilled value valid at the event time but ingested afterward wasn't actually knowable then, so it still leaks future information.
traps.instead: Constrain the as-of join by both event time and availability (ingested_at <= event time).

flows.name: Point-in-time-correct training join
flows.note: For each labeled event the training API joins feature values as-of that event's timestamp from the timestamped offline store — never a future value — so the model trains on what it would actually have seen.

flows.name: Async training-dataset job + manifest
flows.note: A 1B-row PIT join is a multi-TB warehouse scan, so dataset generation is an async job, not a blocking call. Submit returns a jobId and estimate; the join runs admission-controlled; the API publishes a manifest pinning the exact feature versions and source watermark; the model polls until the dataset URI is ready and consumes it.

messages.label: publish manifest: feature versions + source watermark

steps.title: 5. Online Serving and Train/Serve Consistency
description: At inference, the model fetches features for an entity from the online store via the serving API in single-digit ms. The critical correctness property: these online features must be computed by the same logic as the offline features used in training — otherwise train/serve skew silently degrades the model.

The shared registry definition is what guarantees this: the same transform spec drives both offline (batch) and online (stream) computation, so 'user_7d_clicks' means the same thing in both. Avoid reimplementing the transform in serving code. The request pins feature versions (or a model feature set), so the model reads exactly the versions it trained on.

Serving reads are simple key lookups (entity -> feature vector), done as a single multi-get to stay in the latency budget; the hard work (computing the features) happened upstream. Keep the online store in-memory/low-latency for the QPS.

What makes the partial-vector behavior defensible is the model feature set behind modelFeatureSet=ranker@v7. The serving layer resolves that handle into a pinned policy before it reads the online store: a list of members, each with feature_id, pinned feature_version, required vs optional, a safe default_value, and a per-feature max_staleness_ms. So 'continue, drop, or fail closed' isn't decided ad hoc — it's the feature set's declared policy. For example, user_7d_clicks might be required with max_staleness_ms=60000, while user_country is optional with a default of 'unknown'.

The serving contract is then explicit about partial results. The response returns per-feature metadata (version, updatedAt, source = fresh/last_known/default) plus 'missing' and 'stale' lists. A miss, or a value past its policy's max_staleness_ms, falls back to that feature's default or last-known value and is flagged. The inference service then applies the set's fallback policy — continue, drop an optional feature, or fail closed / use a fallback model if a required feature is stale — rather than silently scoring on a bad vector.

view.caption: The model fetches features for inference from the online serving API, which reads the latest values from the low-latency online store.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you guarantee the 'user_7d_clicks' the model sees at serving equals what it was trained on?

concepts.definition: Identical feature logic (from one definition) used for both training and serving.
concepts.whyItMatters: Eliminates skew — the silent killer of production ML quality.
concepts.example: Same count_7d transform computes the offline and online value.

concepts.definition: A named handle (ranker@v7) the serving layer resolves into pinned feature versions plus per-feature policy: required vs optional, a safe default, and a max staleness.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes partial-vector behavior a declared contract — the server knows which feature may be defaulted and how stale each may be, instead of the model guessing at request time.
concepts.example: ranker@v7: user_7d_clicks required, max_staleness 60s; user_country optional, default 'unknown'.

whyNow: Consistency is the whole point of a feature store; it's what the registry + dual store exist to deliver, and the most common ML-systems pitfall.

recap.before: Serving logic could diverge from training.
recap.after: Identical logic both sides; low-latency serving.
recap.newRisk: Online features must be kept fresh, or serving uses stale values.

flows.name: Online feature serve at inference
flows.note: At inference the model fetches an entity's latest features from the online store in single-digit ms via the serving API; the values were computed by the same registry logic used offline (no skew).

flows.name: Partial vector: missing / stale feature handling
flows.note: Serving resolves ranker@v7 into its pinned policy, then reads the online store. user_7d_clicks (required, max_staleness 60s) is stale and user_country (optional, default 'unknown') is missing. The required feature being stale triggers the set's fallback policy (fallback model); the optional one is filled from its default. Both are flagged so the inference service never scores silently on a bad vector.

steps.title: 6. Materialization and Freshness
description: The online store must reflect recent data. Materialize features into it: batch jobs refresh slowly-changing features on a schedule; stream processing updates fast-changing features (recent activity) in near-real-time from event streams.

Freshness is per-feature: 'user lifetime purchases' can lag by hours; 'clicks in the last 5 minutes' must be seconds-fresh. The materialization layer picks batch vs stream per feature based on its freshness requirement, balancing cost against staleness.

Materialization needs an operational contract so it fails loudly, not quietly. Jobs are scheduled and tracked through states (scheduled -> running -> checkpointed -> committed); online writes are idempotent, keyed by (feature, version, entity, event_ts) so retries and replays don't double-count; stream jobs persist checkpoints/source offsets and use event-time watermarks so late events update the right history slice; and a batch refresh writes behind a publish boundary so a half-finished refresh never becomes visible. Backfills (a new feature, or a corrected definition) run as admission-controlled jobs with progress and rollback, and only bind to models once their offline history and online values agree. A large backfill and live stream materialization compete for the same compute and online write bandwidth, so the control plane applies backpressure: it throttles or isolates backfills (a separate resource pool, rate caps) so they never starve the streaming path and breach a feature's freshness SLO.

Monitoring closes the loop with concrete metrics and alert thresholds: last-update age vs the feature's freshness SLO, stream consumer lag, feature null-rate spikes, distribution drift, online/offline skew delta (served value vs offline recompute), stale-read count, and materialization failure rate — catching silent degradation before it hurts the model.

view.caption: Batch compute materializes into the online store while stream compute writes fresh features directly, and the monitoring/drift component watches freshness on the online store and skew against the offline store.

options.name: Per-feature batch vs stream (default)
options.description: Each feature is materialized into the online store by either nightly batch or a stream job depending on its freshness need, with monitoring on freshness and skew.
pros: Each feature picks its path by freshness need: nightly batch for 'lifetime purchases', stream for 'clicks in last 5 min'.
Spends compute only where freshness matters, keeping cost down.
Monitoring tracks per-feature freshness, drift, and online/offline skew to catch silent degradation.
cons: Two compute paths (batch + stream) to build, operate, and keep semantically identical.
Per-feature freshness config is one more thing to get right and review.
Stream and batch implementations of the same transform can drift if not derived from one definition.

view.caption: A materialization job loads batch features and stream compute writes real-time ones into the online store, with monitoring tracking per-feature freshness.

options.name: Batch-only materialization
options.description: Scheduled batch jobs are the only path that refreshes the online store, with no streaming infrastructure for any feature.
pros: One compute path: scheduled batch jobs refresh the online store; no streaming infrastructure.
Simplest to operate and reason about; cheap for slowly-changing features.
Deterministic, reproducible refreshes aligned with offline computation.
cons: Online values are as stale as the schedule — 'clicks in last 5 min' is impossible.
Round-the-clock recompute of all features wastes compute when most rarely change.
Real-time models (fraud, recommendations on recent activity) are unservable.

view.caption: Only the batch path feeds the online store via the materialization job, with monitoring watching freshness on the online store.

options.name: Stream-only (everything real-time)
options.description: Every feature is computed and updated from event streams in near real time, with a single streaming compute paradigm and no batch path.
pros: Every feature is updated from event streams in near real-time — uniformly fresh.
No staleness windows; single compute paradigm.
Best for systems where most features are activity-driven.
cons: Computing inherently historical aggregates (lifetime totals) in a stream is wasteful and hard.
Always-on stream compute costs more than periodic batch for slow features.
Bootstrapping a new feature's history still needs a batch backfill anyway.

view.caption: Stream compute alone writes every feature into the online store in real time, with monitoring watching freshness.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which features can be refreshed nightly in batch, and which must be updated within seconds?

concepts.definition: Batch for slow features, streaming for fast ones, loaded into the online store.
concepts.whyItMatters: Meets each feature's freshness need without paying streaming cost for everything.
concepts.example: Nightly batch for lifetime stats; streaming for last-5-min counts.

concepts.definition: Tracking freshness age, stream lag, null-rate, distribution drift, online-vs-offline skew delta, stale-read count, and materialization failure rate against thresholds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Detects silent ML degradation from stale/skewed features.
concepts.example: Alert when online user_7d_clicks skew delta vs offline recompute exceeds threshold, or stream lag breaches the freshness SLO.

whyNow: Freshness + monitoring make the online store trustworthy; per-feature materialization is the cost/freshness lever and the operational reality.

recap.newRisk: Thousands of features, high serving QPS, and bulk training joins must all scale.

flows.name: Idempotent batch materialization with publish boundary
flows.note: A batch job checkpoints progress, writes idempotently keyed by (feature, version, entity, event_ts), then atomically publishes the refresh so serving never sees a half-finished update. A late event updates the correct history slice by event_ts, not arrival time.

failureDrills.scenario: A large backfill competes with live stream materialization for compute and write bandwidth.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Without backpressure the backfill saturates the online store's write path, stream consumer lag climbs, and fast features (e.g. clicks-in-last-5-min) breach their freshness SLO while the backfill runs — silent staleness on the features that need freshness most. With admission control the control plane caps/isolates the backfill so the streaming path keeps its bandwidth; the backfill simply takes longer, tracked by progress, and freshness SLOs hold.
failureDrills.mitigation: Admission control + separate resource pool / rate caps for backfills; prioritize stream materialization; alert on consumer lag vs SLO; pause or slow a backfill automatically when live freshness is threatened.

steps.title: 7. Scale, Governance, and Reliability
description: Scale the two stores independently: the online store (KV/in-memory) shards by entity for ms reads at high QPS; the offline store (warehouse/columnar) scales for bulk historical scans. Compute (batch + stream) scales with feature count and data volume.

Governance operates the registry's versioned contract at thousands of features and ~100 teams: ownership and approval on definition changes, version pinning (models keep the versions they trained on), lineage (tracked in all three directions — source-to-feature, feature-to-feature for derived features, and feature-to-model), and reuse (a popular feature is computed once and serves many models). Because feature stores hold sensitive user attributes and behavior-derived signals, governance must be enforced, not just annotated. Enforcement lives on the read paths: every serving and training request is authorized against an access_policy keyed by (team/model purpose, feature classification) before the online store or PIT join runs, and every read or definition change is written to an audit_event. Per-feature classification (public/internal/sensitive/PII) drives those checks. Retention and deletion bound the data: historical values age out on a schedule, and a user-deletion request propagates to both offline history and online values.

Reliability: the registry and offline store are authoritative; the online store and materialized values are derived and rebuildable (re-materialize from the offline store/sources). Serving degrades gracefully (default/last-known values) if a feature is missing, rather than failing inference.

view.caption: The online serving API reads the online store and the training data API joins the offline warehouse, both serving the model from the registry-governed feature stores.

steps.decisionPrompt: An engineer changes a feature's definition. How do you avoid breaking models already trained on the old version?
whyNow: Closing on scale + governance + reliability shows the feature store works across many teams/models and stays correct as definitions evolve.

recap.after: Independently-scaled stores, versioned/governed features, rebuildable online state.
recap.newRisk: Definition changes and materialization lag — bounded by feature versioning and rebuildable derived stores.

bottlenecks.issue: Online serving QPS for hot entities.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Shard online store by entity; replicate and cache hot keys; in-memory tier; single multi-get per request to bound fanout.
Version features; models pin versions; compatibility check + deliberate migration before retiring a version.
Alert on consumer lag vs freshness SLO; scale stream consumers; serving flags affected features as stale.
TTL online values; partition + age out offline history; propagate deletion requests to both stores.
Classify features; authorize serving/training reads against access policy on the read path; audit every read and definition change; deny-and-flag rather than silently returning.

failureDrills.scenario: The online store loses a shard.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads for entities on that shard miss; serving returns default/last-known values flagged source != fresh, with the affected features listed as stale/missing so the inference service can drop optional features or use a fallback model for critical ones. The freshness SLO for those features is reported violated while rematerialization runs, and the model is notified via the response metadata rather than getting a silent bad vector.
failureDrills.mitigation: Online store as rebuildable derived state: re-materialize the shard from the offline store/sources with tracked progress; replicas absorb traffic; graceful serving fallback throughout.

failureDrills.scenario: A model requests a sensitive (PII) feature its team isn't approved to use.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The serving/training read is authorized against the access_policy before any store access. The (model purpose, feature classification) pair has no allow rule, so the request is denied — that feature is omitted with a not-authorized reason rather than silently returned — and the attempt is written to an audit_event. The model proceeds without it (if optional) or fails closed, never scoring on data it isn't cleared for.
failureDrills.mitigation: Classification on every feature; policy check on the read path (not just registry annotation); audit log of allow/deny; explicit approval workflow to grant access deliberately.

failureDrills.scenario: A user exercises their right to be deleted.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: A deletion_request for the entity key is recorded and propagates to both stores: offline history rows for that entity are tombstoned/purged and online values are deleted, with the request's status advancing pending -> offline_done -> online_done -> complete. Future training sets exclude the entity; serving returns missing (handled by per-feature defaults) for it. Audit retains that the deletion happened without retaining the deleted values.
failureDrills.mitigation: Entity-keyed deletion that fans out to offline partitions and online keys; retention/TTL policy that ages out history anyway; status tracking so an incomplete deletion is visible and retried.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Feature Store
finalDesign.description: Engineers define each feature once in a central registry as a versioned contract (source + transform, lifecycle, owner). That single definition drives both batch (offline/historical) and stream (online/fresh) computation, written to a dual store: an offline warehouse of values timestamped with both event and ingestion time and a low-latency online KV store of latest values. Training is an asynchronous job: a point-in-time-correct as-of join on the offline store (no leakage, respecting late data) that publishes a manifest pinning the exact feature versions and source watermark it read, so datasets are reproducible and auditable. Serving reads the online store in ms using the same feature logic (no train/serve skew); a model feature set resolves into a pinned policy (required/optional features, defaults, max staleness) so the per-feature metadata and missing/stale flags drive a declared fallback, not an ad-hoc guess. Models pin the feature versions they trained on. A materialization layer keeps the online store fresh per-feature (batch vs stream) with checkpointed, idempotent jobs and a publish boundary, and applies backpressure so backfills never starve stream freshness; monitoring tracks freshness age, lag, drift, and online/offline skew. Stores scale independently; governance is enforced on serving and training reads (classification-based access policy, audit, lineage, retention/deletion), not just documented in the registry. The online store is rebuildable derived state with graceful serving fallback.

view.caption: An ML engineer defines features in the registry, which drives batch and stream compute into the offline and online stores, served to the model via the training and serving APIs with monitoring across both.

functional.how: Offline store + async point-in-time join API publishing a versioned, auditable dataset manifest.

nonFunctional.how: One definition -> both paths; serving resolves a pinned model feature set (required/optional, defaults, max staleness).
As-of joins on timestamped offline store.

Versioned contracts with lifecycle/approval; classification-based access policy enforced on serving/training reads, audit of reads/changes, three-way lineage, and retention/deletion propagated to both stores.

tradeoff: A managed feature platform handles registry, retrieval APIs, materialization hooks, and sharing faster, but the definition DSL, storage integration, and release model become vendor-shaped.
Feast, Hopsworks, or a custom registry keeps compute and store choices open, but the team must operate serving, lineage, permissions, and backwards-compatible feature versioning.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed feature store with built-in registry, retrieval, materialization, and monitoring can remove much of the custom control-plane code from steps 2, 5, and 7.

technologyChoices.concern: Offline historical store and table format
tradeoff: Warehouse-native storage is simplest for point-in-time SQL joins, governance, and elastic scans, but can become expensive when every training run rescans multi-TB history.
Open lakehouse tables on object storage keep history portable across Spark, Trino, and Flink jobs, but require more work on compaction, partition design, schema evolution, and access control.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A warehouse with time travel and managed partition pruning reduces the amount of custom snapshot and manifest logic needed for point-in-time training jobs.

technologyChoices.concern: Online serving store for latest feature values
tradeoff: In-memory stores give the best p99 for small hot vectors, but the 1-2 TB online set quickly turns into an expensive replica-memory problem.
Wide-column and durable KV stores handle larger entity-key shards and high write rates economically, but require careful multi-get batching, hot-key mitigation, and stale/default policy.
makesIrrelevant: A managed autoscaling KV that resizes partitions and replicates across zones removes much of the hand-sharding and failover logic from the online store.
Native TTL support removes a separate stale-value cleanup job for expired online features.

technologyChoices.concern: Batch compute for PIT joins, backfills, and daily features
tradeoff: SQL engines are efficient for simple aggregates and PIT joins when partitioning is good, but complex feature code often escapes pure SQL.
Spark/Beam-style jobs support reusable transforms, backfills, and feature test runs, but need cluster sizing, retry policy, shuffle tuning, and quota isolation from production materialization.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A serverless batch engine with autoscaling and per-job isolation removes most static cluster capacity planning for training-data generation.

tradeoff: A durable log plus event-time processor gives replay, watermarks, checkpointed state, and seconds-fresh features, but it is a continuously running system with nontrivial operational cost.
Queue-triggered functions are simpler for light enrichment or fanout, but are weaker for large keyed windows, late data, and exactly-once materialization.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed stream processing with checkpoints and replay reduces custom offset bookkeeping for materialization jobs.

technologyChoices.concern: Source ingestion, CDC, and connector layer
tradeoff: CDC connectors are the right input for operational features because they preserve source ordering and change boundaries, but schema drift and deletes must be modeled explicitly.
Batch pulls from warehouses are simpler and cheaper for daily aggregates, but cannot meet second-level freshness SLOs.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed connector with schema history, retries, and dead-letter routing removes a large amount of bespoke ingestion glue.

technologyChoices.concern: Training dataset jobs and output manifests
tradeoff: Persisting a manifest with feature versions, source snapshot, watermark, output URI, and row count makes a 1B-row training dataset reproducible and auditable.
A simple output path is faster to build but leaves models unable to prove which feature definitions and source snapshots produced their training data.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A platform registry that versions datasets and model artifacts can replace a custom training_dataset_job manifest store.

technologyChoices.concern: Serving API runtime and inference integration
tradeoff: A long-lived gRPC service gives predictable low latency, request hedging, connection pooling, and model-feature-set policy enforcement close to the online store.
Serverless endpoints are easier to operate for moderate traffic but cold starts and per-request concurrency controls can fight the p99 under 10 ms serving target.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed feature-serving endpoint can remove custom API scaling, policy resolution, and multi-get batching code.

tradeoff: Airflow/Dagster-style DAGs are natural for scheduled materialization and large backfills, but need admission control so backfills do not starve fresh streams.
Temporal/Step Functions-style workflows are stronger for long-running retries and publish boundaries, but add a programming model beyond SQL and batch jobs.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A workflow engine with durable retries, checkpoints, and per-queue concurrency limits removes custom backfill leases and retry tables.

technologyChoices.concern: Governance, lineage, access control, and audit
tradeoff: Policy-as-code and lineage tools make feature classification, approved model usage, and deletion propagation enforceable rather than just documented.
Provider-native governance integrates cleanly with warehouse and lake permissions, but cross-cloud or self-hosted sources often still need a neutral catalog or policy layer.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Provider-native fine-grained access control and audit logging can replace a custom allowlist table for many serving and training reads.

technologyChoices.concern: Freshness, drift, skew, and data quality monitoring
tradeoff: Infrastructure metrics catch stream lag and online-store saturation; feature-quality checks catch null spikes, distribution drift, and train/serve skew.
Embedding data-quality rules in every feature definition improves ownership, but increases authoring friction and requires a review workflow.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed feature store with built-in materialization and feature monitoring removes much of the custom freshness dashboarding from step 6.

say: Confirm the goal: consistent features for both training and serving.
Pin train/serve consistency and point-in-time correctness as the hard goals.
Note low online latency vs bulk offline reads.
Central registry: define each feature once.
Dual offline/online stores from the same definition.
Online serving with identical logic (no skew).
Tradeoffs: freshness vs cost, batch vs stream, definition-change versioning.
Mention lineage and feature reuse/governance as extensions.

expectations: Serves features online and computes them for training.
May reimplement logic per path (skew) and miss point-in-time.
Reasons about train/serve skew and leakage precisely.
Discusses batch-vs-stream freshness/cost and monitoring deeply.

followUps: How exactly does a point-in-time join avoid leakage, and what does the offline store need to support it?
How do you detect and prevent train/serve skew in production?
How do you decide batch vs streaming for a given feature?
How do you version a feature whose definition changes, without breaking trained models?
How would you support feature reuse and lineage across many teams?

links.groupDescription: Core feature-store concepts, open-source platforms, managed services, and architecture papers.
links.title: Overview | Feast: the Open Source Feature Store
links.why: Shows a concrete feature-store architecture: registry, offline and online stores, materialization, point-in-time historical retrieval, and online serving.

links.groupDescription: Core feature-store concepts, open-source platforms, managed services, and architecture papers.
links.title: feathr-ai/feathr
links.why: Open-source feature platform with LinkedIn production heritage; useful for studying feature definitions, point-in-time training joins, sharing, and online deployment.

links.groupDescription: Core feature-store concepts, open-source platforms, managed services, and architecture papers.
links.title: The Hopsworks Feature Store for Machine Learning
links.why: Treats the feature store as a DBMS for ML and digs into point-in-time joins, offline query services, online retrieval, latency, throughput, and benchmarks.

links.groupDescription: Core feature-store concepts, open-source platforms, managed services, and architecture papers.
links.title: Managed Geo-Distributed Feature Store: Architecture and System Design
links.why: Architecture paper from a managed feature-store product team; frames duplicated features, reuse, version control, online/offline skew, leakage, and geo-distributed design.

links.groupDescription: Core feature-store concepts, open-source platforms, managed services, and architecture papers.
links.title: Create, store, and share features with Feature Store
links.why: Managed-service reference for online, offline, and combined storage modes, batch/stream ingestion, and feature discovery/sharing.

links.groupDescription: Core feature-store concepts, open-source platforms, managed services, and architecture papers.
links.title: About Vertex AI Feature Store
links.why: Managed feature-store reference for feature registry objects, feature views, BigQuery-backed sources, online serving clusters, and production quotas.

links.groupDescription: Production ML platforms and feature systems described by teams that built or operated them.
links.title: Meet Michelangelo: Uber's Machine Learning Platform
links.why: Early production feature-store case study with a shared feature catalog, offline HDFS/Hive/Spark paths, online low-latency serving, daily feature updates, and roughly 10,000 features.

links.groupDescription: Production ML platforms and feature systems described by teams that built or operated them.
links.title: Bighead: Airbnb's End-to-End Machine Learning Platform
links.why: Company ML platform reference for Zipline-style feature management, training pipelines, deployment, online inference, and offline/online consistency concerns.

links.groupDescription: Production ML platforms and feature systems described by teams that built or operated them.
links.title: Open sourcing Feathr - LinkedIn's feature store for productive machine learning
links.why: Explains the organizational motivation for a shared feature platform at LinkedIn: hundreds of models, thousands of features, reuse, production deployment, and faster feature iteration.

links.groupDescription: Production ML platforms and feature systems described by teams that built or operated them.
links.title: Distributed Time Travel for Feature Generation
links.why: Practical case study for reconstructing historical feature values from snapshots so offline experiments use data as it would have existed at prediction time.

links.groupDescription: Production ML platforms and feature systems described by teams that built or operated them.
links.title: Building Riviera: A Declarative Real-Time Feature Engineering Framework
links.why: Shows how DoorDash used Flink SQL, Kafka, and a Redis-backed feature store to build accessible, reusable, isolated real-time feature pipelines.

links.groupDescription: Production ML platforms and feature systems described by teams that built or operated them.
links.title: Introducing Fabricator: A Declarative Feature Engineering Framework
links.why: Describes a centralized feature workflow product, including feature lifecycle, observability, declarative pipelines, and scaling to hundreds of features and large daily feature volumes.

links.groupDescription: Production ML platforms and feature systems described by teams that built or operated them.
links.title: How Pinterest Accelerates ML Feature Iterations via Effective Backfill
links.why: Detailed production backfill design covering time-correct alignment, feature leakage risks, version control, rollback, data-quality statistics, and compute-cost reduction.

links.groupDescription: References for avoiding leakage, joining historical features correctly, and building reproducible training datasets.
links.title: Point-in-time feature joins
links.why: Concrete API reference for timestamp lookup keys, lookback windows, automatic point-in-time joins, and publishing time-series features to online stores.

links.groupDescription: References for fresh feature computation, online serving, backfills, streams, and durable materialization.
links.title: Materialize Features
links.why: Explains steady-state materialization, backfills, batch and stream feature views, and publishing precomputed features to online or offline stores.

links.groupDescription: References for fresh feature computation, online serving, backfills, streams, and durable materialization.
links.title: Stateful Stream Processing
links.why: Stream-processing reference for keyed state, fault tolerance, and checkpointed operators that keep fresh feature aggregates correct and recoverable.

links.groupDescription: References for fresh feature computation, online serving, backfills, streams, and durable materialization.
